Thinks if she feels well and her last ultrasound was normal, she doesn't need ongoing cancer screening.

Believes removing visible skin/oral growths eliminates the underlying risk and that internal cancers would show obvious symptoms early.
Worries that PTEN/Cowden is contagious or could be 'caught' through sharing bathrooms or intimacy, and avoids discussing it with partners.
Thinks stress and diet alone can 'turn off' the gene problem, so she prioritizes supplements over surveillance.
Believes genetic testing results are always definitive and unchangeable; if one test was 'negative years ago,' she assumes she is cleared forever.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 34-year-old woman. You are vague and a bit evasive, worried about costs, insurance, and missing work. Start with: you found a new lump in the front of your neck over the last couple of months and it seems to be getting bigger. If asked about other symptoms, you may slowly add: intermittent hoarseness, more fatigue, and tiny bumps around your mouth you’ve had for years, plus you’ve “always had a big head/needed bigger hats.” Only if directly asked, mention: feeling something stuck when swallowing, occasional blood in stool, heartburn/bloating, painful mouth sores, recurring skin tags/rough patches, irregular periods, a breast lump that changes with your cycle, and harder-to-control blood sugars (genetic diabetes). Do not volunteer your history of polyps, prior breast biopsies, missed thyroid ultrasound, headaches/pressure, gum/tongue growths, uterine procedure, or family history unless specifically probed.
Express misconceptions: it’s probably thyroid/weight changes; blood is hemorrhoids; mouth bumps are acne/toothpaste; hoarseness is allergies/reflux; fatigue is diabetes.
If the clinician is empathetic, you soften briefly, admit fear of cancer and finances, but still minimize and avoid details about sexual bleeding/genital wart unless asked directly.

ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO every other day
cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 2000 IU PO daily
confounders: Multinodular goiter/thyroid nodules causing low TSH and elevated thyroglobulin that can mimic thyroid malignancy
Hyperprolactinemia with possible pituitary microadenoma vs medication/stress-related elevation, complicating evaluation of menstrual irregularity
Iron-deficiency anemia from heavy menstrual bleeding, which can obscure constitutional symptoms and complicate cancer-related fatigue assessment
Overweight/insulin resistance and dyslipidemia confounding glycemic control and cardiovascular risk assessment in MODY

expected_lab_results.Free T4: often normal; may be abnormal if thyroid dysfunction present
expected_lab_results.Thyroid peroxidase (TPO) antibody: typically negative unless autoimmune thyroiditis coexists
expected_lab_results.Thyroglobulin antibody: typically negative unless autoimmune thyroiditis coexists
expected_lab_results.Thyroglobulin (tumor marker, with thyroi: not a screening test; may be elevated with benign nodules/goiter or thyroid cancer
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin A1c: variable; assess for diabetes/insulin resistance if clinically indicated
expected_lab_results.Fasting plasma glucose: variable; assess for diabetes if clinically indicated
expected_lab_results.C-peptide (fasting): typically normal or elevated if insulin resistance present

non_pharmacological: Genetic counseling and PTEN testing (if not already done)
Cancer surveillance: annual thyroid ultrasound starting at diagnosis
Breast surveillance: annual breast MRI starting age 30; add annual mammography starting age 30-35
Endometrial cancer surveillance: educate on abnormal uterine bleeding; consider endometrial biopsy based on symptoms/risk; discuss risk-reducing options
Colon surveillance: colonoscopy starting age 35 (earlier if symptomatic/family history) and repeat every 5 years (shorter interval if polyps)
Renal surveillance: renal ultrasound every 1-2 years starting age 40
Lifestyle risk reduction: weight management, exercise, limit alcohol, avoid tobacco
Family cascade testing and screening for at-risk relatives

Explains key diagnostic features in plain language: severe unilateral orbital/supraorbital/temporal pain with ipsilateral autonomic symptoms (e.g., tearing, conjunctival injection, rhinorrhea, ptosis) and/or restlessness, occurring in attacks with circadian/circannual periodicity.

Elicits red flags and excludes secondary headache features (e.g., first/worst thunderclap, new neurologic deficit, fever/meningismus, cancer/immunosuppression, age >50 new headache, persistent continuous pain, atypical duration, change in pattern).

Avoids inappropriate acute medications for cluster headache (e.g., opioids) and does not recommend oral triptans as sole acute therapy due to slower onset (acceptable only as an adjunct with correct counseling).

Uses clear safety-net instructions: seek urgent care for decreased urine output, lethargy/confusion, bleeding, severe abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, or signs of dehydration.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as "Sinding-Larsen-Johansson disease" and frames it as an overuse/traction-related condition often seen in active adolescents.
Explains expected clinical features: anterior knee pain localized to the inferior patellar pole/patellar tendon origin, worsened by running/jumping/squatting and improved with rest.
Communicates that the condition is typically self-limited and improves with conservative care; sets realistic recovery expectations (often weeks to months) and emphasizes gradual return to sport.
Explains treatment in plain language and must include both "activity modification" and "NSAIDs" as options for symptom control (with appropriate cautions).

Performs required safety checks before any prescribing: asks about medication allergies (critical) and contraindications to NSAIDs (critical; e.g., history of GI bleed/ulcer, kidney disease, anticoagulant use, uncontrolled hypertension, asthma/NSAID sensitivity, pregnancy where relevant).

If prescribing is discussed, also considers drug interactions (non-critical) such as anticoagulants, antiplatelets, SSRIs/SNRIs, systemic steroids, lithium, methotrexate, ACEi/ARB+diuretic combination.
Avoids unsafe or inappropriate management for L0: no opioid recommendations, no intra-articular steroid injection advice, no antibiotics, and no urgent surgery claims.

Provides red-flag guidance and escalation criteria: inability to bear weight, large effusion, fever, erythema/warmth, locking/true instability, night pain/systemic symptoms, significant trauma, or persistent/worsening pain despite several weeks of conservative care.
Uses patient-centered communication: checks understanding, invites questions, and provides a brief plan for follow-up/return precautions.
